name: Replacement Migration UN
description: The 'Replacement Migration' theory, often associated with conspiracy theories, centers around the idea that the United Nations (UN) has a deliberate policy to encourage mass immigration from developing countries into developed nations to 'replace' or dilute the existing populations and cultures of those nations. Proponents often cite a 2000 UN Population Division report titled 'Replacement Migration: Is it a Solution to Declining and Ageing Populations?' as evidence. This report explored demographic trends and projected the levels of immigration needed to maintain the size of the working-age population in several developed countries. Conspiracy theorists interpret this report as a blueprint for a secret agenda to undermine national identities and cultures. Mainstream demographers and the UN itself maintain that the report was a purely academic exercise exploring potential demographic scenarios and not a policy recommendation. They argue that it was a study, not a plan, and that the UN has no authority or mechanism to enforce such a policy. Critics of the theory emphasize the complex factors driving migration, including economic opportunities, political instability, and personal choices, and they denounce the theory as a form of xenophobia and racism, often pointing to its overlap with white nationalist ideologies. The theory is often amplified online and used to justify anti-immigrant sentiment, despite lacking credible evidence of a coordinated UN-led effort.
subtitle: UN's Alleged Population Replacement Agenda

claims.claim: The UN has a secret plan to replace native populations with immigrants.
claims.criticism: The report was a demographic study, not a policy recommendation. The UN has no authority to enforce such a plan.

claims.claim: Mass immigration is intentionally engineered to dilute Western cultures.
claims.evidence: Increased immigration rates in Western countries, perceived cultural changes.
claims.criticism: Immigration is driven by complex factors, including economic opportunities and personal choices. Cultural change is a natural process.

claims.claim: The UN is promoting a 'one-world government' by undermining national identities.
claims.evidence: UN's advocacy for global cooperation and international agreements.
claims.criticism: The UN's role is to facilitate international cooperation on specific issues, not to create a centralized world government.

keyFigures.name: Peter Brimelow
keyFigures.role: Founder of VDare, a website promoting anti-immigration views.

keyFigures.name: Jean Raspail
keyFigures.role: Author of 'The Camp of the Saints,' a novel often cited by proponents of the theory.

keyFigures.name: Joseph Chamie
keyFigures.role: Former Director of the UN Population Division, who has publicly criticized the theory's misrepresentation of the UN report.

mythologyConnections: The Tower of Babel (narrative of forced dispersion and cultural fragmentation)
The concept of 'flood myths' (destruction and replacement of existing populations)

timeline.event: Publication of the UN 'Replacement Migration' report.
The theory begins to gain traction online in far-right circles.
Increased visibility and mainstream discussion of the theory, often associated with anti-immigration movements.
